aliases: Caesar salad with croutons and parmesan

english: Caesar salad with croutons and parmesan

region_of_origin: Mexico (Tijuana) / United States; popularised internationally as Italian-American
food_subgroup: Composed salads with dressing, cheese, and croutons

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Caesar salad exists; A0CKP (composite salad dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Caesar salad found in the clinical terminology; condition-level food coding not applicable here.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Caesar salad is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous bed of romaine lettuce, Caesar dressing, parmesan shavings, bacon bits, and croutons.

nutrient_source.title: Caesar salad — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exactly matches this restaurant-style Caesar salad with bacon bits and croutons. Values are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for romaine lettuce (NDB 11251), Caesar salad dressing (NDB 04623), parmesan cheese (NDB 01033), bacon (NDB 10124), and plain croutons (NDB 18239), combined in proportions consistent with a standard restaurant Caesar salad (approximately 55% lettuce, 20% dressing, 10% croutons, 8% parmesan, 7% bacon). Energy cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry 'Salad, Caesar' (FDC ID 168409).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed green salad with dressing and croutons
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Caesar salad exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 38 is estimated based on the analogous composite salad with croutons and dressing category. The dominant glycemic contributors are croutons (GI ~72 for white bread croutons) and the overall low carbohydrate density of the dish. Weighted composite GI estimated at approximately 38 (low), consistent with mixed salad dishes containing moderate crouton content. GL per 300 g serving = (38 × 9.2 × 300/100) / 100 = 10.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from Caesar dressing, parmesan cheese, and bacon
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with Caesar salad due to approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1140 mg per standard 300 g restaurant serving), which represents nearly 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; requesting dressing on the side and limiting bacon can reduce sodium intake meaningfully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Caesar salad has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 38 and a glycemic load of approximately 10.5 per standard serving, making it a generally suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, portion size of croutons should be monitored as they are the primary glycemic contributor, and the high-fat dressing should be accounted for in overall energy management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from bacon, parmesan, and mayonnaise-based dressing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Caesar salad contains approximately 2.8 g saturated fat per 100 g primarily from bacon bits, parmesan cheese, and mayonnaise-based dressing; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consider requesting reduced dressing and omitting bacon to lower saturated fat and sodium intake, while benefiting from the romaine lettuce's folate, vitamin A, and vitamin C content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Caesar salad (Italian: Insalata di Cesare) is a classic composed salad originating in Tijuana, Mexico, created by Italian-born restaurateur Caesar Cardini in the 1920s, and now widely served in Italian-American and international restaurants worldwide. The dish consists of crisp romaine lettuce leaves tossed in a rich, tangy Caesar dressing made from an emulsion of mayonnaise, garlic, lemon juice, and capers, then topped with shaved or grated parmesan cheese, crispy bacon bits, and crunchy croutons. The texture profile combines the fresh crunch of romaine with the creamy coating of the dressing, the salty umami of parmesan and bacon, and the bread-like crunch of croutons. Nutritionally, Caesar salad provides moderate energy (approximately 150 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from cheese and bacon (approximately 5.8 g per 100 g), and notable fat content (approximately 10.5 g per 100 g) from the dressing and cheese. It is a good source of vitamin A (from romaine lettuce), folate, calcium, and vitamin C. The sodium content is relatively high due to the dressing, parmesan, and bacon, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 38 (low), making it broadly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when crouton portions are moderated. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served in Italian restaurants and Western casual dining establishments as a starter or light main course.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Caesar salad?
Is Caesar salad good for diabetics?
Is Caesar salad high in fat?
Can I eat Caesar salad if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry for this specific restaurant-style Caesar salad with bacon bits and croutons exists. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for individual components. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database for similar mixed salad dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the composite methodology is well-documented.
notes: Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Vegetables' as Caesar salad is a vegetable-dominant composed dish. The canonical English name 'Caesar Salad' is used as food_name; the Italian name 'Insalata di Cesare' is preserved as the romanized_local name and in aliases. This is an Italian-cuisine dish served at Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore (Boat Quay, Singapore) per field survey source data. Multilingual local_names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable for this Italian dish and are set to null; the G6 multilingual rule applies to Singaporean dishes, not Italian cuisine dishes. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite source for this dish type. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not -1 sentinel values. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite source for Caesar salad components. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Caesar salad is available in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (38 × 9.2 × 300) / 10000 = 10.5. Price data from field survey data (SGD 22.35) is preserved in source metadata but not included in the nutrient record as it is restaurant-specific.

Built composite nutrient estimate from USDA SR Legacy component entries with documented proportions.
Estimated GI at 38 using University of Sydney GI Database analogous composite salad methodology.
